channel.title: Jiǎoluò Shūwū · 角落書屋
channel.description: Notes on Chinese language and civilisation — characters, vocabulary, grammar, history, philosophy, and the world they shaped.

item.title: 产 · to produce, give birth, property, output
item.description: From birth to bounty: a character that spans biological generation and industrial output, linking the miracle of birth to the logic of production and wealth.

item.title: 告 · to tell, to announce, to report
item.description: A mouth aimed upward: the character for delivering words to authority — and the root of advertising, accusations, and warnings.

item.title: 广 · wide, broad, to spread
item.description: A radical and a word: the cliff-shelter that became the prefix for everything expansive — from Guangzhou to Guangdong to radio broadcasts.

item.title: 品 · article, grade, character, to taste
item.description: Three mouths stacked: a character about multiplicity, evaluation, and the discerning assessment of quality — from product grades to moral character.

item.title: 图 · map, diagram, to plan, to seek
item.description: A character for diagrams, maps, intentions, and plots — anything that represents the world at one remove, or seeks to reshape it.

item.title: 之 · of, going, this — the classical connective
item.description: The most-written character in classical Chinese — a possessive particle, pronoun, and verb fossilized into pure grammar.

item.title: 笔画 · strokes — the building blocks of Chinese characters
item.description: Every Chinese character is built from a small set of standard strokes written in a prescribed order. Stroke count determines dictionary order; stroke order determines how a character looks when written by hand.

item.title: 部首 · radicals — the indexing keys of Chinese characters
item.description: Every Chinese character belongs to a radical family. Radicals are the organizing principle of dictionaries, the structural DNA of characters, and the fastest shortcut to guessing what an unfamiliar word means.

item.title: 产品 · product, manufactured good
item.description: The standard Chinese word for product — what production yields and commerce moves. From factory floor to digital marketplace, 产品 names the output of organized human effort.

item.title: 拼音 · the romanization system for Mandarin
item.description: Pinyin is the official romanization of Mandarin — the system that maps Chinese sounds to the Latin alphabet. It is a pronunciation guide, not a writing system, and it does not replace characters.

item.title: 生产 · to produce, production, manufacturing
item.description: The foundational act of economic life — and, in China's Marxist political vocabulary, the act from which all social relations flow. Life gives rise to production; production sustains life.

item.title: 声调 · the four tones of Mandarin
item.description: Mandarin is tonal: the same syllable said at four different pitches means four different things. Tone is not emphasis — it is part of the word.

item.title: 楚辞 · Songs of Chu
item.description: The shamanistic southern voice that gave Chinese literature its second pole — visionary, romantic, and inconsolably exiled.

item.title: 昆曲 · Kun Opera
item.description: The oldest surviving form of Chinese opera — refined to such perfection over four centuries that it nearly disappeared from its own beauty.

item.title: 鲁迅 · Lu Xun
item.description: The writer who looked at traditional Chinese society and saw cannibalism — and the complicated, unresolvable legacy he left behind.

item.title: 诗经 · Book of Songs
item.description: China oldest poetry anthology — three hundred poems that Confucius said no educated person could afford to ignore.

item.title: 水墨画 · Ink-Wash Painting
item.description: Where the brush meets philosophy — five tones of ink, the art of leaving space, and landscapes that are not about landscape at all.

item.title: 宋词 · Song Dynasty Lyric
item.description: The poem that sings — fixed tune patterns, named meters, and three masters who defined what the Chinese lyric could feel.

item.title: 文房四宝 · Four Treasures of the Study
item.description: Brush, ink, inkstone, and paper — the four instruments through which a scholar thought, wrote, painted, and demonstrated who he was.

item.title: 篆刻 · Seal Carving
item.description: The art of the name — bronze to stone, dynasty to dynasty, two schools of carvers, and the small red square that authenticates everything.

item.title: 爱 · love, to love, to cherish
item.description: Love in all its forms — romantic, familial, and the Confucian ethic of universal benevolence.

item.title: 变 · to change, to transform
item.description: The character at the heart of the I Ching, the dynastic cycle, and the modern vocabulary of reform — change as the one constant.

item.title: 不 · no, not, negation
item.description: The great negator — the most used character in Mandarin and the foundation of all denial.

item.title: 茶 · tea
item.description: The character that gave the world its word for tea — a grass over wood over man, a plant that shaped civilization, and the locus of China's richest material culture.

item.title: 长 · long; to grow; chief
item.description: A person with flowing long hair — one character, two readings: cháng (long) and zhǎng (to grow, elder), the richest dual-reading in Mandarin.

item.title: 吃 · to eat
item.description: To eat — and the extraordinary range of Chinese life encoded in a single food verb.

item.title: 打 · to hit, to strike; the most versatile verb in Mandarin
item.description: To hit — and a hundred extensions: make a call, play a sport, pack a bag, get an injection. The most productive light verb in Chinese.

item.title: 大 · big, great, large
item.description: Big, great, large — a person with arms wide open, the original measure of magnitude.

item.title: 地 · earth, ground, land
item.description: Heaven above, earth below — the second power of the cosmos, and the root of every word about place, ground, and belonging.

item.title: 电 · electricity, electric, telegram
item.description: Lightning descending from a rain cloud — a character that jumped from describing the weather to naming the force that defines modern civilization.
